title: Naan bread
subtitle: Soft, blistered flatbread cooked under a very hot grill
excerpt: A yeasted flatbread leavened with yoghurt as well as yeast, cooked on a preheated heavy tray under the highest grill setting you can manage. The blisters and the char are the point. Ready in under two hours start to finish.
sourceNotes: Naan bread is a leavened flatbread with roots across South and Central Asia, particularly in Indian, Pakistani, and Afghan culinary traditions. The domestic version cooked under a grill rather than in a tandoor is a British-Indian household staple. The yoghurt in the dough gives a characteristic slight tang. This recipe follows the established method, adapted for a home grill.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze cooked naan flat in a bag, with baking paper between each one. Reheat from frozen in a dry frying pan or under a hot grill for 1 to 2 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough can be made up to 24 hours ahead and left to rise slowly in the fridge. Bring to room temperature for 30 minutes before shaping.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: highest grill setting; preheat a heavy baking tray or cast-iron skillet in the oven for 15 minutes before cooking

glossaryTerms.definition: Stretch a small piece of naan dough between your fingers. If it stretches to a thin, near-transparent membrane without tearing, the gluten is sufficiently developed. If it tears immediately, knead for another 2 minutes and test again.

content.text: The domestic naan is not the tandoor naan. It will not reach the charred, chewy perfection of bread cooked at 480 degrees C on a clay wall. But a naan baked on a very hot tray under the full grill of a domestic oven comes close: blistered on top, soft in the centre. The trick is temperature. Preheat the tray until it is very hot, then cook quickly. Apply the
to the kneaded dough before the first rise.

Combine 400 g flour, 4 g yeast and 6 g salt in a large bowl. Add 150 g yoghurt and 90 ml lukewarm water. Mix to a soft, slightly tacky dough. Knead on an unfloured surface for 8 minutes until smooth. Apply the windowpane test. Shape into a ball, return to the bowl, cover with a damp cloth and leave at room temperature for 1 hour until doubled.

Place a heavy baking tray under the grill on the highest shelf. Turn the grill to its maximum setting and preheat for 15 minutes. Divide the proved dough into 6 equal pieces, about 100 g each. On a lightly floured surface, roll each piece into an oval about 25 cm long and 3 to 4 mm thick. Traditional naan is teardrop-shaped: roll one end thinner than the other. Domestic ovens drift by 20 degrees C or more; an oven thermometer confirms the grill is at full heat.

Working quickly, lay one or two naan on the preheated tray. Slide back under the grill. Cook for 2 to 3 minutes until large blisters form and the surface is charred in patches. The naan does not need turning. Brush immediately with melted butter and keep warm in a low oven while you cook the rest. Reheat the tray for 1 minute between batches.

items.problem: Naan cooks slowly and comes out dry rather than blistered
items.solution: The tray was not hot enough. Preheat for a full 15 minutes at maximum grill setting and reheat between batches. The naan should blister within the first 30 seconds of contact with the tray.

items.problem: Dough is very sticky and hard to roll
items.solution: Yoghurt brands vary in thickness. If the dough is too wet, add flour a tablespoon at a time until it is manageable. Chill in the fridge for 15 minutes before rolling if it springs back during shaping.
